title: NATO Open Spirit 2026 Mine Countermeasures Exercise Begins in Baltic Sea
summary: NATO launched the Open Spirit 2026 exercise in the Baltic Sea, deploying approximately 400 military personnel including SNMCMG1 to clear World War-era explosive remnants and practice multinational mine countermeasures through May 13.
contents:
## NATO Open Spirit 2026 Commences in Baltic Sea

### Exercise Overview
NATO's Open Spirit 2026 exercise commenced on May 1 in the Baltic Sea, with operations continuing through May 13. The exercise focuses on detecting and neutralizing mines and other dangerous objects remaining on the seabed from World War I and World War II.

### Force Composition
- Approximately 400 military personnel participating
- Ships from multiple NATO nations deployed
- NATO's Standing NATO Mine Countermeasures Group One (SNMCMG1) leading multinational operations
- Specialized mine clearance divers conducting identification and neutralization tasks

### Operational Significance
The Baltic Sea ranks among the most heavily mined bodies of water in the world, with thousands of unexploded munitions still present from past conflicts. These remnants pose ongoing risks to commercial vessels, fishermen, and underwater infrastructure.

### Dual-Purpose Training
Beyond the practical seabed clearance mission, Open Spirit practices interaction between different national fleets, divers, and headquarters. The exercise standardizes procedures and tactics across participating nations. This year's maneuvers run alongside Sweden's Aurora 2026 army exercises, enhancing regional coordination and adding strategic significance.

### Alliance Context
The exercise reinforces NATO's commitment to maritime security in the Baltic region amid heightened strategic competition. Russia's Foreign Ministry has accused Western countries of planning to turn the Baltic Sea into a training ground.
